fr:mainmatter.html:p: A scalable Cache coherence organization for systems too large for a shared bus. Each memory address has a home node; the home node maintains a directory entry listing which caches currently hold copies and in what state. Coherence transactions are point-to-point messages routed via the directory rather than broadcasts.
Compared to Snoop-based coherence, directory protocols trade a level of indirection (3 hops instead of 2 for some transactions) for vastly reduced bandwidth — making them viable at hundreds of cores.

Centralized vs. distributed. A single central directory is a bottleneck; production systems distribute it. Each processor's local DRAM has its own directory tracking the addresses physically present in that DRAM. Every physical address uniquely identifies its home node.
Reducing directory size. The naive directory is a sharer list per cache line:

html:ul.html:li: One-hot bitmap (256 nodes → 32 bytes per entry): 50% of memory consumed by metadata.
Limited-sharer encoding: most workloads have ≤4 sharers; track those exactly, fall back to broadcast for the uncommon case.

Sparse directories. Cache the directory entries themselves. An entry missing from the directory cache means nobody has a copy. On eviction from the directory cache, invalidate the corresponding lines.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Because directory transactions take multiple hops, intermediate states are visible — transient states like \mathrm {MI}^{A} (modified-to-invalid, waiting on acknowledgment). Concurrent transactions race; the protocol must handle every combination correctly.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: L has a dirty copy and wants to write back to H. R concurrently sends a read to H. R's state \mathrm {S}^{D} means "shared, waiting for data".
Never use NACKs in a directory protocol — they cause starvation.
See Implementing Directory-based MESI in Murphi for a worked implementation in Murphi, including the channel-separation arguments that prevent protocol-level deadlock.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Prefetching. Fetch data before requested to hide coherence latency.

Upgrade-miss detection. In migratory sharing (read then write), a naive read-miss followed by write-miss costs two coherence transactions. Detect the pattern and transition straight to a "migratory E" state on read.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: The same optimization hurts producer-consumer sharing. There, the right move on read-miss is to downgrade the previous holder rather than invalidate, so subsequent readers can fetch from the prior writer.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A Cache coherence family where every cache watches (snoops) a shared bus. When one cache issues a coherence transaction (e.g. requests permission to read or write a line), every other cache sees it and updates its local state accordingly. No central directory needed; the bus itself is the coordination mechanism.
The simplest snoop-based protocol is valid-invalid:

html:ul.html:li: Invalid + load → BusRd to gain read permission; transition to valid.
Invalid + store → no transition (write-through assumed; the bus carries the write).
Valid + store → BusWr to notify other caches; they invalidate.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Write-update coherence, the variant where stores update other caches' copies instead of invalidating them, works for very small bus-based systems but does not scale: every write reaches everyone, and copies of data the snooper isn't using pollute its Cache replacement policy.
Snoop-based coherence relies on a shared physical bus, which limits it to roughly 8 cores. Beyond that, switch to Directory-based coherence.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Snoop-based SMPs use the Inclusion property so that snooping at L2 suffices — every L1 line is also tracked at L2.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: In a Multicore system where every core has its own Cache hierarchy, the same memory address can live in multiple caches simultaneously. A cache coherence protocol is the mechanism that keeps those copies consistent — propagating writes, invalidating stale copies, and preserving two invariants:

html:ol.html:li: Single-writer invariant: at any moment, at most one core has permission to write a given line. No other core can read or write it concurrently.
Data-value invariant: all cores see the same order of updates to a given line.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Two organizational families:

html:ul.html:li: Snoop-based coherence — every cache watches a shared bus and reacts to other cores' traffic. Scales to ~8 cores.
Directory-based coherence — a structure tracks who has a copy of each line; coherence messages are point-to-point. Scales to hundreds of cores at the cost of an extra hop.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The concrete state machines are protocols like MSI protocol, MESI protocol, MOESI protocol, and DEC Firefly, each adding states to handle more cases or to optimize specific sharing patterns. A worked implementation of directory-based MESI in Murphi lives in Implementing Directory-based MESI in Murphi.

A Cache coherence family where every cache watches (snoops) a shared bus. When one cache issues a coherence transaction (e.g. requests permission to read or write a line), every other cache sees it and updates its local state accordingly. No central directory needed; the bus itself is the coordination mechanism.
The simplest snoop-based protocol is valid-invalid:

html:ul.html:li: Invalid + load → BusRd to gain read permission; transition to valid.
Invalid + store → no transition (write-through assumed; the bus carries the write).
Valid + store → BusWr to notify other caches; they invalidate.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Write-update coherence, the variant where stores update other caches' copies instead of invalidating them, works for very small bus-based systems but does not scale: every write reaches everyone, and copies of data the snooper isn't using pollute its Cache replacement policy.
Snoop-based coherence relies on a shared physical bus, which limits it to roughly 8 cores. Beyond that, switch to Directory-based coherence.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Snoop-based SMPs use the Inclusion property so that snooping at L2 suffices — every L1 line is also tracked at L2.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: In a Multicore system where every core has its own Cache hierarchy, the same memory address can live in multiple caches simultaneously. A cache coherence protocol is the mechanism that keeps those copies consistent — propagating writes, invalidating stale copies, and preserving two invariants:

html:ol.html:li: Single-writer invariant: at any moment, at most one core has permission to write a given line. No other core can read or write it concurrently.
Data-value invariant: all cores see the same order of updates to a given line.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Two organizational families:

html:ul.html:li: Snoop-based coherence — every cache watches a shared bus and reacts to other cores' traffic. Scales to ~8 cores.
Directory-based coherence — a structure tracks who has a copy of each line; coherence messages are point-to-point. Scales to hundreds of cores at the cost of an extra hop.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The concrete state machines are protocols like MSI protocol, MESI protocol, MOESI protocol, and DEC Firefly, each adding states to handle more cases or to optimize specific sharing patterns. A worked implementation of directory-based MESI in Murphi lives in Implementing Directory-based MESI in Murphi.
This will also serve as a bit of notes from A Primer on Memory Consistency and Cache Coherence
Syntactical things I need to remember from Murphi docs. Most things are straightforward, but there are a few things to note:
Declarations. A declaration is a variable or a collection of variables that are used in the rules.
Rules. A rule is a transition from one state to another

html:ul.html:li: Each rule can optionally have an initial condition required to be fired
We can easily define rules for all the processors or collection of n things with ruleset.
For our use case, rules are used to define

html:ol.html:li: Receiving a request from the network

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Startstates. A startstate is the intial configuration for all our declarations.

html:ul.html:li: Must assign a value to every global variable

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Invariants. An invariant is something that Murphi checks to ensure is true at all possible configurations of the system.
Overview straight from the docs. Repeat forever:

html:ol.html:li: Find all rules whose conditions are true in the current state. (i.e. conditional expressions are true, given the current values of the global variables).
Choose one arbitrarily and execute the action, yielding a new state.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
The first thing we need to do is understand all the things that can go awry.
First in the valid-invalid protocol example we were given they only have two virtual channels. However, we need 3 channels to handle, request messages, forwarded messages, and response messages.
Let us briefly understand why this is the case. Detecting a deadlock is equivalent to detecting a cycle in a dependency graph, so it is sufficient to show a cyclic dependency.
If we issue a GetS this goes to the directory, giving us an edge N1 -> D.
Then the directory issues a Fwd-GetS to the ownder, giving us the chain N1 -> D -> N2
The owner then needs to send data to the requester, giving us the chain N1 -> D -> N2 -> N1.
Namely, the data must go through the same channel as all of the requests depending on it.
To avoid this, we partition into 3 channels:
Request: GetS, GetM, PutM
Forwarded: Fwd-GetS, Fwd-GetM, PutAck, Inv
Response: InvAck, Data
We directly enumerate these in our MessageType as MessageType: enum { GetS, -- request for shared copy GetM, -- request for modified (exclusive) copy PutS, -- evict shared copy PutM, -- evict modified copy (w/ data)
FwdGetS, -- forwarded GetS to owner FwdGetM, -- forwarded GetM to owner Inv, -- invalidation request
Data, -- data response InvAck, -- invalidation acknowledgment PutAck -- put acknowledgment }; Cache controller. We will start with MSI cache controller, given by the following table:
Notably, we are missing some of the messages in the figures below. We can handle these based on the AckCount ProcState: Record state: enum { P_Invalid, P_Shared, P_Modified, --stable states PT_IS_D, --sent GetS, waiting for Data PT_IS_DI, --sent GetS, got Inv, waiting for Data then go to I PT_IM_AD, --sent GetM (from I), waiting for Data and InvAcks PT_SM_AD, --sent GetM (from S), waiting for Data and InvAcks PT_IM_A, --got Data for GetM, waiting for remaining InvAcks PT_MI_A, --sent PutM, waiting for PutAck PT_SI_A, --sent PutS, waiting for PutAck PT_II_A --waiting for PutAck, already invalidated }; val: Value; ack: AckCount; -- tracks InvAck count End;
Kellen Kanarios2026324Remark The load, store, and replacement all only alter the states of the coherence protocol for the permanent states. Therefore, to simulate the system it is sufficient to only implement the rules that initiate these state transitions i.e. evict in modified state or load from invalid state.
Directory controller. Now for the directory controller:
Kellen Kanarios2026324Remark We actually end up adding a second transient state to the directory controller M^D. This is to handle the PutM, GetM race described below.
Weird Race Conditions
Kellen Kanarios2026324Problem When implementing the PutM rule to downgrade from modified to invalid. We must NOT get rid of the data until we have been acknowledged, so that we can still service forward requests. This is a transition to MI^A. However, we can still receive the PutAck causing us to transition to I before servicing the Fwd-GetM request. This is lost data.
Kellen Kanarios2026324Solution We must add a M^D state to the directory controller. In this state, if we get a PutM request from a node that is not our current owner we stall and wait for data to arrive. For M^D to transition to M, we must send the data back to the directory even though we are doing a three-hop, so that the directory knows the data has also been forwarded to the new owner. It is unclear to me the best way to do this without this state.
Kellen Kanarios2026324Problem
The above solution causes a deadlock, where two processors both store miss and one load misses. The "winning processor" of the store will be the second one. The first one will get the data from the directory transitioning from IM^{AD} \to IM^{A}, but the second one will then trigger a Fwd-GetM overwriting the first.
The load miss transitions to IS^D. However, when it receives an Inv it stalls according to the above figure. This blocks the Fwd-GetM from reaching the other winning processor because they are on the same VC. Thus, we have a deadlock where the owner never gets the data.
Kellen Kanarios2026324SolutionWe need to add another transient state not included in the figure. Namely, we add II^{A} that says we were in IS^{A}, but we got invalidated and now we are waiting for our PutAck before we transition to I.
Kellen Kanarios2026324Remark When the home directory is in M and we get a GetS we cannot clear owner as directed by figure. Otherwise in S^D, we will not be able to tell if the PutM is from the owner and stall, so we only remove the owner once we have received the data.

Aside from the modifications discussed above, the rest was straightforward and we now are ready to move on to MESI.
Cache controller. We will start with MESI cache controller, given by the following table:
Design choice 1. Since we allow immediate upgrade to M, without forwarding, if E upgrades to M, then the value in the directory sent to the next GetS / GetM would not have updated value. It seems we must either
not allow E to silently upgrade to M and then we do not need forwarding, or
allow E to silently upgrade to M and treat E as M basically and forward everything. Notably, neither of these have to write back on PutE, but we will go with 1 because that is how it is indicated in the figure.
Design Choice 2. In order to indicate that the directory received the first GetS we introduce a ExclusiveData message. Upon receiving, ExclusiveData in IS^D we transition to E.
Kellen Kanarios2026324Remark Once we have sent the PutE, or the PutM EI^A and MI^A are functionally the same. I include both for completeness but verified that transitioning directly to MI^A from E works the same.
Directory controller. We start with the analagous table.
Similar to the cache controller, we also add a E^D state for completion that can be absorbed into M^D introduced in the MSI protocol.
Weird Races.
Kellen Kanarios2026324ProblemFirst processor does GetS and is in IS^D but the Home directory sends it exclusive data and is now in E. Then second processor does GetM and Home issues Fwd-GetM to processor one, which arrives before data.
Kellen Kanarios2026324SolutionNeed to stall on Fwd-GetM and Fwd-GetS in IS^D until data arrives
Kellen Kanarios2026324ProblemAn even crazier race happens when we get invalidated before the Fwd-GetM or Fwd-GetS arrive. Due to our previous design decision, we transition to II^A, where if we receive a PutAck we transition to invalid. This then loses the needed forward data.
Kellen Kanarios2026324Example The timeline:
Proc_1 performs a load miss → IS^D
Home receives first GetS from Proc_1 → E and sends ExclusiveData to Proc_1
Proc_2 issues GetM → IM^AD
Home sends Inv to Proc_1
Home forwards Fwd-GetM to Proc_1
Proc_1 receives Inv → II^D
Proc_1 receives ExclusiveData → I Data is lost
Kellen Kanarios2026324Solution We once again have to use the ExclusiveData message. We can then see that we got ExclusiveData in II^D and transition to E instead of I. E will then correctly handle the Fwd-GetM or Fwd-GetS.
We are left with the following state diagrams for the cache and directory controllers respectively
